personality_voice.book_baseline: Pettigrew in the books is defined by a consistent cluster of traits: cowardice as a core identity (not just a flaw but his defining characteristic), sycophantic flattery directed at whoever holds power, desperate self-preservation above all else, and a wheedling, squeaky, pleading voice. He is physically rat-like in appearance and manner. His speech is characterized by repetition, fragmented sentences, appeals to sentiment ('my old friends,' 'kind boy,' 'sweet girl'), and rapid shifts between groveling and sulky resentment. He shows flashes of sulkiness when cornered (the 'I found you' exchange with Voldemort in GoF, the 'I am not your servant' exchange with Snape in HBP). He is not purely passive - he has moments of cunning (capturing Bertha Jorkins, cutting off his own finger, escaping as a rat). His cowardice is explicitly ideological: he chose the winning side, not out of belief but out of survival calculus.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The films preserve the core personality well. In PoA, Pettigrew's transformation scene captures his squeaky voice, darting eyes, groveling at Ron, Hermione, and Harry, and his pleading appeals to sentiment. The line 'Haven't I been a good friend? A good pet?' is directly preserved. His physical rat-like quality is maintained. In GoF, his trembling, wheezing servility to Voldemort during the resurrection is faithfully rendered. In DH1, his brief appearance at Malfoy Manor preserves his subservient role. The sulky resentment seen in HBP's Spinner's End scene ('I am not your servant!') is entirely absent from the films, as that scene is cut.
personality_voice.difference: The films lose the sulky, resentful edge Pettigrew shows in HBP when he briefly pushes back against Snape's condescension. They also lose the GoF chapter's extended dialogue showing his nervous attempts to suggest alternatives to Voldemort's plan - his voice 'tumbling from him in a rush, as though he was forcing himself to say this before he lost his nerve.' The film GoF compresses his dialogue to near-silence during the Riddle graveyard sequence. The cunning dimension (Bertha Jorkins capture, the self-amputation plan) is referenced but not dramatized in a way that shows his agency.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality - cowardice, sycophancy, groveling, pleading, rat-like manner, squeaky voice - is well preserved across PoA and GoF film scenes. The loss of the HBP Spinner's End scene removes a nuanced moment of sulky resentment, and the GoF compression removes the extended nervous dialogue showing his internal conflict about the plan. These are secondary traits rather than core ones. Minor penalty applied.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Pettigrew serves multiple narrative functions: (1) the revelation that upends the assumed history of PoA, (2) the instrument of Voldemort's resurrection in GoF, (3) a living embodiment of the theme that cowardice has consequences, (4) the carrier of the life-debt that Dumbledore predicts will matter, (5) a minor but structurally important presence in HBP (Spinner's End) and DH (Malfoy Manor, where his moment of mercy leads to his death). His agency includes: cutting off his own finger to fake death, surviving 12 years as a rat, capturing Bertha Jorkins, performing the resurrection ritual, and the crucial moment in DH where his silver hand hesitates and kills him.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The films preserve his role as the PoA revelation and the GoF resurrection instrument. His DH appearance at Malfoy Manor is retained, including the silver hand killing him. The HBP Spinner's End scene is cut entirely, removing his role as Snape's reluctant servant and the dynamic that shows his diminished status among Death Eaters.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The Spinner's End scene (HBP) is entirely absent. This removes Pettigrew's function as a presence in Snape's household and the brief moment where he asserts 'I am not your servant' - a small but revealing act of agency. More significantly, the GoF film compresses his role during the graveyard sequence: his extended nervous dialogue with Voldemort about using a substitute wizard is cut, reducing his agency from a character who actively (if fearfully) tries to negotiate his situation to a near-silent functionary. The film also does not dramatize the Bertha Jorkins capture, which in the books is presented as a rare moment of Pettigrew's own initiative.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The core narrative functions are preserved: PoA revelation, GoF resurrection, DH mercy/death. The Spinner's End omission removes a secondary narrative function. The GoF compression reduces his agency during the graveyard from active (nervous negotiation) to passive (silent execution of tasks). These are meaningful reductions but not eliminations of his role.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Pettigrew's motivations are layered and explicit in the books. His primary motivation is survival at any cost - he chose Voldemort because Voldemort was winning and he feared death. His internal conflict is between residual loyalty/guilt toward his old friends and his overriding self-preservation instinct. In GoF, he shows a secondary motivation: a desire for recognition and reward from Voldemort ('I found you... I brought you Bertha Jorkins'). In DH, the silver hand's hesitation suggests a buried impulse of mercy or residual human feeling that his conscious will cannot sustain. The books make explicit through Sirius's accusation that Pettigrew 'never did anything for anyone unless you could see what was in it for you' - his motivation is purely transactional.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The PoA film preserves the survival motivation clearly: 'I didn't mean to! The Dark Lord, you have no idea the weapons he possesses! Ask yourself what you would have done, Sirius.' The transactional self-interest is preserved in the groveling scenes. The DH silver hand moment is retained. However, the GoF film removes the extended dialogue where Pettigrew tries to suggest using a substitute wizard - which in the books reveals his motivation to escape the dangerous task and possibly desert Voldemort. The sulky resentment in HBP ('I found you') that reveals his desire for recognition is entirely absent.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film loses the GoF scene where Pettigrew's motivation to escape Voldemort's service is made explicit through his nervous suggestion of alternatives. It also loses the HBP scene where his desire for recognition ('I found you, I brought you Bertha Jorkins') is shown. These are not the primary motivation but they add texture to his internal conflict - the tension between wanting to escape and being trapped.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The primary motivation (survival, self-preservation, transactional loyalty) is intact. The secondary motivations (desire for recognition, desire to escape) are partially lost. The internal conflict is simplified but not fundamentally changed. The DH mercy moment is preserved. Moderate simplification warrants a minor penalty.

character_arc.book_baseline: Pettigrew's arc across the series is: (1) loyal Marauder and friend, (2) coward who betrays his friends to Voldemort, (3) 12 years hiding as a rat, (4) revealed and escapes, (5) resurrects Voldemort at great personal cost (hand), (6) diminished servant at Spinner's End, (7) moment of involuntary mercy in DH leads to his death. The arc is one of irreversible moral degradation punctuated by a single involuntary redemptive impulse that kills him. The books establish his starting point (the Marauder photograph, the OotP memory of him as an anxious hanger-on) and trace his decline. Dumbledore's prediction that the life-debt will matter is fulfilled in DH.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The films capture the key arc points: the revelation in PoA, the resurrection in GoF, the DH death. The starting point (young Pettigrew as Marauder) is not shown in the films - the OotP Pensieve scene showing young Pettigrew watching James and Sirius with awe is absent. The HBP diminished-servant stage is absent. The arc therefore jumps from PoA revelation directly to GoF resurrection to DH death, skipping the intermediate stage of his degraded existence as Voldemort's servant.
character_arc.difference: The OotP Pensieve scene showing young Pettigrew as an anxious, admiring hanger-on is absent from the films (the OotP film omits this entirely). This removes the visual establishment of his starting point - the contrast between the eager young man and the groveling traitor. The HBP Spinner's End stage is absent. The arc is compressed but the key beats (betrayal established through dialogue, resurrection, mercy/death) are present.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc's starting point is not dramatized in the films - we never see young Pettigrew. The intermediate degradation stage (HBP) is absent. The arc is therefore incomplete: it lacks the full trajectory from eager follower to traitor to diminished servant to involuntary redeemer. The key turning points of GoF and DH are present, but the arc feels truncated rather than complete. The Dumbledore life-debt prediction and its fulfillment are both present, which preserves the arc's thematic resolution.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Pettigrew's key relationships: (1) Sirius Black - former friend turned pursuer, defined by Sirius's contempt and Pettigrew's terror; (2) Voldemort - master/servant with Voldemort's cruelty and Pettigrew's abject servility; (3) James Potter - the friend he betrayed, whose memory haunts the PoA confrontation; (4) Lupin - former friend who is more measured than Sirius but equally condemning; (5) Harry - the son of the man he betrayed, toward whom he shows a complex mix of guilt, manipulation, and the life-debt. The Snape relationship in HBP is a secondary but revealing dynamic showing Pettigrew's diminished status.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Sirius/Pettigrew dynamic is preserved in PoA - Sirius's contempt and Pettigrew's terror are both present. The Voldemort/Pettigrew dynamic is preserved in GoF. The Harry/Pettigrew dynamic (life-debt) is preserved in both PoA and DH. The Lupin/Pettigrew dynamic is present but compressed in PoA. The James Potter connection is referenced in dialogue. The Snape/Pettigrew relationship (HBP) is entirely absent.
key_relationships.difference: The Snape/Pettigrew relationship is entirely absent due to the Spinner's End scene being cut. This removes a dynamic that reveals Pettigrew's status among Death Eaters and his resentment at being treated as a servant. The Lupin/Pettigrew dynamic is compressed - in the books, Lupin's measured questioning of Pettigrew ('I must admit, Peter, I have difficulty in understanding why an innocent man would want to spend twelve years as a rat') is more developed than in the film. The James Potter connection is referenced but not dramatized (no Pensieve scene).
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The primary relationships (Sirius, Voldemort, Harry) are preserved in their essential dynamics. The Snape relationship is entirely absent. The Lupin relationship is compressed. The James connection is referenced but not shown. These are meaningful losses but the most important dynamics are intact.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: The books give Pettigrew considerable complexity: (1) the sulky resentment that surfaces briefly even in his degraded state ('I found you'); (2) the extended GoF dialogue showing his nervous attempts to negotiate with Voldemort, revealing both his fear and his desire to escape; (3) the HBP scene showing him as Snape's reluctant domestic servant, briefly asserting dignity before being crushed; (4) the OotP Pensieve showing him as a young man - anxious, admiring, chewing his nails, hoping to copy from neighbors - establishing the texture of his inadequacy before the betrayal; (5) the DH silver hand moment, which is the most complex beat: an involuntary mercy that his conscious will cannot sustain, killing him; (6) Dumbledore's observation that 'there was a bit of regret there, somewhere' - the books insist on his humanity even while condemning him.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The films preserve the groveling complexity of the PoA confrontation, the physical horror of the GoF resurrection (including his revulsion at carrying Voldemort's body), and the DH silver hand moment. The film PoA captures his manipulation attempts across multiple characters (Ron, Hermione, Harry). However, the OotP Pensieve scene is absent, the HBP Spinner's End scene is absent, and the GoF extended dialogue is compressed.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The OotP Pensieve scene is entirely absent - this is the only moment in the films where we would see young Pettigrew and understand the texture of his inadequacy and hero-worship. Without it, the audience has no visual baseline for who he was before the betrayal. The HBP scene is absent, removing the brief flash of resentful dignity. The GoF compression removes the extended nervous negotiation. Dumbledore's post-PoA observation about 'a bit of regret' is present in the book but absent from the film (the Dumbledore/Harry conversation after PoA is cut). The life-debt conversation with Dumbledore, which frames Pettigrew's complexity thematically, is absent from the PoA film.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The absence of the OotP Pensieve scene, the HBP Spinner's End scene, and the Dumbledore life-debt conversation removes significant nuance. The audience never sees young Pettigrew, never sees his brief assertion of dignity, and never hears Dumbledore's framing of his complexity ('there was a bit of regret there, somewhere'). The DH silver hand moment partially compensates, but the overall texture of his complexity is noticeably reduced. Cap 3 applies: a core emotional beat (Dumbledore's framing of the life-debt and its thematic meaning) is absent from the films.

lost_or_transferred_material: OotP Pensieve scene: young Pettigrew shown as anxious, nail-chewing, admiring hanger-on watching James and Sirius - establishes his starting point and the texture of his inadequacy

HBP Spinner's End scene: Pettigrew as Snape's reluctant domestic servant, briefly asserting 'I am not your servant!' before being crushed - shows his resentful dignity and diminished status among Death Eaters
GoF extended Riddle House dialogue: Pettigrew nervously suggesting using a substitute wizard instead of Harry, revealing his desire to escape Voldemort's service and his fear of the plan

GoF Voldemort's speech to Death Eaters: Voldemort's account of Pettigrew finding him in Albania and capturing Bertha Jorkins - the one moment of Pettigrew's own initiative is narrated but not dramatized
Post-PoA Dumbledore/Harry conversation: Dumbledore's framing of the life-debt ('there was a bit of regret there, somewhere') and its thematic significance - absent from the PoA film
GoF sulky exchange: Pettigrew's 'I found you... I brought you Bertha Jorkins' - his desire for recognition from Voldemort is absent
Lily Potter's letter reference to 'Wormy' visiting - the human texture of Pettigrew before the betrayal
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if a core book function, decision, or emotional beat is transferred or absent): The Dumbledore life-debt framing and the OotP Pensieve scene are absent, removing material necessary to understand Pettigrew's thematic function and complexity. Score held below 75.
comparative.key_observations: The films preserve Pettigrew's core identity - cowardice, groveling sycophancy, rat-like manner, squeaky voice, and the key plot functions of PoA revelation, GoF resurrection, and DH mercy/death - with reasonable fidelity. The PoA confrontation scene is the most faithful section, capturing his pleading manipulation across multiple characters and his physical terror. The DH silver hand moment is preserved. However, the films lose three significant layers of complexity: the OotP Pensieve scene that establishes his starting point as an anxious, inadequate hanger-on; the HBP Spinner's End scene that shows his brief resentful dignity and diminished status; and the GoF extended dialogue that reveals his desire to escape Voldemort's service. The Dumbledore post-PoA conversation framing the life-debt thematically is also absent. The result is a Pettigrew who is recognizable and whose core traits are intact, but who is stripped of the nuance that makes him more than a one-note coward - particularly the moments that insist on his residual humanity and the texture of his inadequacy before the betrayal.
